text: In this video, we present minimally invasive inguinal hernia repair in a patient with history of robotic prostatectomy. The patient was a sixty-three-year-old male who had complaints of bulging masses in the right and left groin regions. He had a history of robotic radical prostatectomy due to prostate cancer. His BMI was twenty-six. His ASA score was two.
Decision was given to perform totally extraperitoneal laparoscopic hernia repair.

The patient is positioned supine under general anesthesia. A transverse infraumbilical incision is made to access the anterior rectus sheath, entering the preperitoneal space. Balloon dissection is performed, followed by insufflation with carbon dioxide. Two 5mm trocars are inserted under direct vision in the midline of the suprapubic region, spaced four centimeters apart.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the space of Bogros, visualizing the iliopubic tract. An indirect inguinal hernia is identified on the patient's left side. The left inguinal region is exposed using blunt and sharp dissection, revealing the hernia sac, which is then dissected from the spermatic cord components and reduced into the abdominal space.

The surgeon pushes down the peritoneum, widening the medial and lateral areas to prepare for mesh placement. The mesh is placed in the retroperitoneal area to cover potential femoral, direct, and indirect hernia sites. Tags are used to fixate the mesh to the pubic tubercle and lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon attempts extraperitoneal dissection of the right inguinal region, but the procedure is hindered by widespread scar tissue and adhesions from the patient's previous radical prostatectomy surgery.

The surgeon performs a transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair. The peritoneal cavity is accessed, and trocars are placed. An incision is made in the peritoneum over the right inguinal area, and the defect is widened to expose the anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon identifies the indirect hernia sac and carefully separates it from the spermatic cord elements before reducing it into the abdominal cavity. Neurovascular structures and the vas deferens are observed to be intact, and the space is prepared for mesh placement.
The surgeon places a biocompatible mesh in the right inguinal region to cover potential hernia areas. Tags are used to fixate the mesh, and the peritoneal defect is closed using barbed sutures.

No additional pathologic conditions were detected in the abdominal cavity. The trochers were then taken out under direct visualization and the operation was complete. The operative time was one hundred and twenty minutes and the estimated blood loss was twenty milliliters. Postoperative period was uneventful and the patient was discharged on postoperative day one.
